caveat: This is about fingerprinting MODELS, not code. Included as a counter-signal showing the applicability limit of provenance-detection: weights-level works, snippet-level does not.

title: Reddit is suing Anthropic for scraping (100K+ accesses), in the presence of deals with Google/OpenAI for $130M
description: Reddit files suit against Anthropic in California Superior Court (June 4, 2025): 100,000+ unauthorized accesses to Reddit since mid-2024, training Claude on posts without a license, continued scraping after public statement about blocking bots. The claim is based on breach of contract and unfair competition, NOT on copyright. Contrast: Google ($60M, 2024) and OpenAI (~$70M, 2025) entered into licensing deals with Compliance API (deletion callbacks, privacy guardrails); Anthropic refused to negotiate. Data licensing = $130M = ~10% of Reddit revenue. In October 2025, Reddit is also suing Perplexity + scrapers (Oxylabs, SerpApi, AWMProxy).
why_important: Core data-licensing layer and strong H7 enhancement. Reddit is not just fighting scraping — it is turning user-generated corpus into a licensed asset ($130M). The legal strategy is indicative: breach of contract instead of copyright, because the copyright on user posts on Reddit is weak. The platform does not monetize its own content, but rather controls access to someone else’s (user’s) content. Reddit — #1 cited source in AI, 3x more often than Wikipedia.

title: Wikimedia: -8% human pageviews YoY due to AI responses; Enterprise as a paid channel
description: Wikimedia Foundation: human pageviews fell ~8% YoY (March-August 2025 vs 2024) after updating bot-detection (part of the 'human' traffic turned out to be stealth bots from Brazil). The reason is that search engines provide AI answers directly, without sending them to the source (60% of Google searches with AI-summary). At the same time, bandwidth from AI bots +50% from January 2024. Wikimedia Enterprise (paid opt-in channel, launched 2021) — Amazon, Google, Meta, Microsoft, Perplexity, Mistral pay for data feeds without overloading servers.
why_important: The ideal OSS↔commons↔publishing bridge. Volunteer infrastructure: AI takes content, distributes load (bandwidth +50%) and takes traffic (-8%), undermining the volunteer→editor→donor funnel. The same economy as curl/Jazzband, but on commons. Wikimedia Enterprise is a parallel to Reddit-deals: turning free knowledge into a paid access channel. Direct illustration D7 (externalization) + H7 (paywall as protection).
caveat: -8% — after retrospective reclassification of bots (not raw measurement). Wikimedia warns that cross-period comparisons should be treated with caution while detection logic evolves. The trend is stable, the exact figure is an estimate.

title: RSL Standard: robots.txt turns from yes/no into a licensing signal (pay-per-crawl/inference)
description: Launch of Really Simple Licensing (RSL) — an open standard that extends robots.txt from 'can/can't crawl' to machine-readable licensing and royalty terms. Models: free, attribution, subscription, pay-per-crawl (pay for each crawl), pay-per-inference (pay for each use in the answer). Startup support: Reddit, Yahoo, People Inc., Internet Brands, Ziff Davis, Fastly, Quora, O'Reilly, Medium. Non-profit RSL Collective as a collective rights organization (co-founder — Eckart Walther, co-creator of RSS). RSL 1.0 — December 10, 2025.
why_important: Structural shift for D9/D10: robots.txt was a binary fence (D5 vendor_transparency), RSL makes it a licensing infrastructure. This is an attempt to build ASCAP/BMI for the web — collective rights org, turning provenance/access into a monetized stream. The strongest strengthening of H7: not the fight against slop, but the transformation of public corpus into an asset. It hits the same enforcement wall (AI companies ignore robots.txt) — but creates a legal basis for lawsuits (like Reddit).

title: Cascade publisher data-deals: AP/OpenAI, Reuters/Meta, News Corp, how to monetize the corpus
description: A wave of licensing deals between publishers and AI vendors is taking shape in an industrial pattern: Google/AP (January 2025, Google’s first AI content licensing deal), Reuters/Meta (October 2024, estimated $25M one-time + $40M for 3 quarters), plus previously News Corp/OpenAI, Axel Springer/OpenAI. In parallel, Tollbit records: sites without AI-licensing deals lost CTR ~3x between Q2 and Q4 2025; even with deals the CTR drops.
why_important: The data-licensing picture completes: the training-data licensing market has formed as a class. It's a counter-movement to 'free scrape' — but only available to large publishers with bargaining power. Small creators and OSS maintainers do not have such a mechanism (part of the H7 asymmetry: only those who have market mass can monetize corpus). RSL is an attempt to give small people the same opportunity through a collective.
caveat: Specific transaction amounts (Reuters/Meta $25M+$40M) are press estimates not confirmed by the parties. The fact of the existence of transactions is reliable, the numbers are approximate.

title: Stockfish v ChessBase: GPLv3 enforcement worked, settlement with SFC as certifier
description: Stockfish (FOSS chess engine, GPL-3.0) won a case against ChessBase GmbH in District Court Munich I (Case 42 O 9765/21). ChessBase distributed derivatives (Fat Fritz 2, Houdini 6) under a proprietary license, violating the GPL. Settlement: ChessBase does not distribute Stockfish for a year, then only in full compliance with GPL-3.0 with notification to users. Software Freedom Conservancy has been appointed a trusted third-party to certify future Corresponding Source releases.
why_important: Counterweight to 'the law is dead' (balances D4/D6). Classic OSS-license enforcement WORKS for direct copying and derivative works. This is H1b_legal in action: law is alive where there is direct use of the code. The contrast with AI-rewrite (Claw-Code), where enforcement stalls — the difference is not in the law, but in provability. Shows the boundary: the GPL enforces against ChessBase, but not against functional AI replication.

title: SFC v Vizio: GPL is both a copyright license and a contract; the third beneficiary can enforce
description: Software Freedom Conservancy is suing Vizio (California state court, October 2021) as a third beneficiary of GPLv2 — not as a copyright holder, but as a buyer of TV on Linux demanding a Corresponding Source. A federal judge in remand ruled: The GPL functions as both a copyright license AND a contract. December 2025 — partial decision on summary adjudication; the tribunal is scheduled for August 2026. If the SFC wins, device buyers will be able to enforce OSS licenses directly.
why_important: The strongest counterweight: expands rather than narrows the scope of OSS-enforcement. GPL as a contract = not only the author, but also the beneficiary user can enforce. This is the opposite of the thesis 'law is powerless'. Balances D4: while AI-rewrite eludes copyright, classic copyleft, on the contrary, is strengthened through contract theory. H1b_legal: the law does not die, it evolves — but towards direct use, not AI-laundering.

title: Google v Oracle (SCOTUS): code 'primarily functional', API reimplementation = fair use
description: US Supreme Court (Google v Oracle, April 2021): reimplementation of 11,500 lines of Java API in Android is fair use. Key wording: computer programs are 'primarily functional', which makes traditional copyright concepts difficult to apply to code. USCO Part 3 report (2025) develops: code at the functional end of the fair-use factor-two spectrum.
why_important: A fundamental precedent for the entire legal framework of the layer. 'Code primarily functional' works both ways: it protects API re-implementation (Google), but also weakens protection against AI-rewrite, where behavior/architecture survives, not expressive detail. Explains WHY Claw-Code is legally murky: if functionality is not protected as strictly as expression, AI porting functionality into another language is legally grayer. This precedent is the root of H1b uncertainty.

title: RIAA (UMG/Sony/Warner) is suing Suno and Udio for training on copyright records
description: The RIAA on behalf of Universal, Sony, Warner files two lawsuits against Suno and Udio (June 24, 2024): unauthorized use of copyright records for training music-generation models. Suno admitted that the training data 'includes essentially all music files of reasonable quality accessible on the open internet'; Udio generates 10 songs/sec (864K files/day). The lawsuit states the threat directly: 'saturate the market with machine-generated content that will directly compete with, cheapen and ultimately drown out genuine sound recordings'. Suno defends itself with fair use (link to Bartz).
why_important: Music legal balance: copyright holders are suing for training, not just removing slop from platforms. Complements H7 with a second side — Spotify/Deezer remove AI-slop (pool protection), labels are suing generators (source protection). The wording of the claim 'drown out genuine recordings' is literally the same economics as AI-slop in OSS/publishing: cheap generation displaces expensive genuine ones.

title: Labels move from lawsuits to licensing: UMG/Udio and Warner/Suno settlements, joint platforms 2026
description: Music suits are split into strategies. UMG settle with Udio (Oct 29, 2025): licensing partnership + joint AI music platform for 2026 with opt-in artist compensation + compensatory legal settlement. Warner settle with Suno (Nov 25, 2025): licensing + acquisition Songkick. Sony does NOT settle — holds on to fair-use ruling (expected summer 2026) as a precedent. Suno filed a motion for summary judgment (March 2026) for fair use with reference to Bartz.
why_important: Branch split LICENSING_VS_ENFORCEMENT in music: the same labels that filed the lawsuit are turning it into a licensing asset (like HarperCollins with books). Confirms H7: the goal is not 'anti-AI', but monetization control. Two of the three majors exchanged the claim for equity+licensing walled-garden; Sony is setting a precedent. This is a musical mirror of how provenance/training turns into a licensed stream.

title: Independent musicians file class actions: the majors' settlement does not protect them
description: Independent artists file separate class actions against Suno and Udio (October 2025), covering all independent artists/songwriters/producers whose works were included in the training. Argument: major-label settlements (UMG/Udio, Warner/Suno) do not protect smaller rights holders — major labels negotiate for themselves, small labels are left with nothing.
why_important: Accurate illustration of H7 asymmetry. Only those with market power (majors, HarperCollins, Reddit) can monetize provenance through licensing. Small creators — independent musicians, OSS maintainers, individual authors — do not have such a mechanism and are forced to sue as a class. Direct parallel: settlement of large people = walled garden for their own, the rest are left behind. This is the same gap as between Reddit ($130M deals) and a solo maintainer.
caveat: Class actions at an early stage (submission Oct 2025), outcome unknown. Included as a signal of structural asymmetry, not as a resolved case.

title: Registry governance: npm/PyPI/RubyGems implement Trusted Publishing (OIDC), sigstore provenance, credential overhaul
description: Registers respond to supply-chain attacks with governance mechanisms according to the OpenSSF standard: Trusted Publishing via OIDC (publishing from CI/CD without long-lived tokens), sigstore provenance attestations, namespace protection (npm scopes, PyPI organizations). npm began revoking legacy tokens on December 9, 2025 (deadline ~February 3, 2026), publisher-side authentication overhaul completed December 2025. Sonatype: 1.2M+ malicious packages blocked in 2025. PyPI after GhostAction (570+ repo, 3,300+ secrets) invalidated tokens and moved everyone to Trusted Publishers.
why_important: Second side D3 (previously only attacks). Registers are not passive victims of slopsquatting, but active governance actors: signing, provenance, quarantine, OIDC. This is D3+D5+D9 governance answer. Balances the supply-chain layer: cheap generation of attacks (D3) meets an institutional technical response (D5 technical_defense).

title: Shai-Hulud worm bypasses Trusted Publishing: valid SLSA provenance on poisoned packages
description: The self-propagating worm Shai-Hulud (fifth wave in 8 months) subverts the governance infrastructure itself: short-lived OIDC tokens of GitHub Actions are intercepted from CI/CD runners and used to publish malicious versions with valid SLSA Build Level 3 provenance. 84 versions in 42 @tanstack/* packages in 6 minutes; in 48 hours — 172 packages, 403 versions (npm+PyPI), 518M cumulative downloads affected. TanStack had everything 'on paper': OIDC trusted publishing, signed provenance, 2FA — the attack worked. Endor Labs: 'provenance was real, packages were poisoned'. Total Shai-Hulud for generations — 796 packages, 132M monthly downloads.
why_important: The critical limit of the governance response. provenance/signing/2FA proves that the package is published by a legitimate publisher — but NOT that the publisher is not compromised. This is a direct parallel to H1/H9: technical provenance (signing) is bypassed by the adversarial method (OIDC theft), just as AI code detection is bypassed by paraphrase. 'The attacker is the legitimate publisher at the time of publication' — provenance collapse in the supply chain. Enhances H9 beyond AI detection.

title: Hachette recalls horror novel Shy Girl after AI allegations — the first book review by a major publisher
description: Hachette (Big Five), after an internal review, stopped sales of the novel Shy Girl by Mia Ballard, concluding that significant parts were generated by AI: the US release was canceled, UK distribution was stopped (~1800 copies sold), the circulation was put under the knife. Trajectory: self-published success (Feb 2025) -> deal with Hachette -> UK release (Nov 2025) -> suspicions of readers on Reddit/Goodreads/YouTube -> NYT verifies the claim of the Pangram AI detector -> review. The author denies personal use of AI, blames the freelance editor, and declares his intention to sue.
why_important: Gate worked AFTER passing all the filters — self-publishing, acquisition by a major, publication. The publisher acts as a platform: pull/cancel/pulp based on SUSPECTION (crowd-detection + commercial AI detector + reputational risk), and not based on a fact proven in court. provenance is blurred to the point of indecisibility (author versus editor). A parallel to OSS: the contribution was accepted, passed review, and then withdrawn due to suspicion — and there is no one to respond.
caveat: The use of AI has not been legally proven: the publisher acted on suspicion (Pangram detector + reader analysis). The author denies and threatens to sue the editor. The region is conditionally US (Hachette US decision + cancellation of the US release), although the publication was UK.

title: WGA after strike 2023: AI is not a writer, AI is not a source material (collective bargaining agreement)
description: After a 148-day strike, the Writers Guild of America established an AI mode in MBAs with AMPTP: neither traditional AI nor GAI are considered a writer, so the material they produce is not literary material; AI-output is not considered a source material (does not undermine copyright credit and separated rights); the company cannot oblige the writer to use AI and is obliged to disclose if the submitted material is AI-generated; The WGA reserves the right to challenge AI learning in the works of authors.
why_important: The first institutional guild regime, where 'AI is not the author' is enshrined not in court or platform policy, but in a collective agreement forced by a strike (which stopped ~$10 billion of production). A precedent for labor agreements in other industries. A direct illustration of a "license of agency in reverse": a collective subject dictates who and how has the right to use the agent in production — something that an individual cannot achieve.
